questions.context:
Geysers are hot springs that periodically erupt, ejecting tall columns of heated water and steam high into the air. Early naturalists in the eighteenth century regarded these fountains merely as curiosities, offering no mechanism for their rhythmic behavior. [■] They assumed that a hidden fire directly boiled the water at the surface, an idea that explained the visible heat yet completely ignored the striking regularity with which the bursts recurred.

This surface-bound guessing gave way during the nineteenth century to a more physical account of the underground plumbing. [■] Investigators proposed that groundwater slowly collects in a narrow subterranean channel, where the weight of the overlying column raises the boiling point far above the normal value. When the deepest water finally flashes into steam, the sudden expansion drives the eruption upward. [■] This model was incomplete, however, because it pictured every geyser as one simple vertical tube.

Modern researchers, equipped with downhole cameras and buried temperature sensors, have substantially revised that tidy picture. [■] They now find that many geysers depend on tangled networks of side chambers that trap pockets of steam and quietly govern the timing of each eruption. Researchers are continuously uncovering how these hidden cavities store and release energy, steadily refining their predictions of when a given geyser will next erupt.

options.A: predict
options.B: control
options.C: delay
options.D: record

questions.context:
Magnetism is a fundamental force that arises from the motion of electric charge, most familiar in the invisible attraction between certain metals. Every electron behaves as a minuscule magnet because it carries both electric charge and an intrinsic property called spin. [■] In most ordinary materials these atomic magnets point in random directions, so their separate fields cancel one another and no overall magnetism can be detected.

This cancellation breaks down in a small group of metals such as iron, nickel, and cobalt. [■] Within these ferromagnetic metals, neighboring atoms lock their spins into the same orientation, forming microscopic regions called domains in which countless electrons align. When an external field nudges these domains to grow and point together, the metal becomes strongly magnetized. [■] Removing the field can leave much of that alignment intact, producing a permanent magnet.

The strength of any magnet therefore depends on how completely its domains can be aligned and then held in place. [■] Engineers exploit this fact by blending elements that resist the random thermal jostling which would otherwise scramble the alignment. Designers now apply these principles when building compact electric motors, dense magnetic data storage, and the powerful coils inside hospital imaging scanners.

questions.context:
Impressionism was a nineteenth-century French painting movement that sought to capture the fleeting effects of light and atmosphere rather than precise, finished detail. Its painters abandoned the smooth surfaces and somber tones prized by the traditional academies. [■] Instead they worked rapidly outdoors, laying down broken strokes of bright, unmixed pigment that a viewer's eye would blend together at a distance.

This technique rested on a deliberate trust in the observer's own vision. [■] Rather than blending green on the palette, an Impressionist might place separate dabs of pure blue and yellow side by side, letting them fuse optically into a livelier hue. [■] Because sunlight shifts constantly through the day, these artists painted quickly, often finishing a canvas in a single session to preserve one exact moment of changing light.

Such radical methods reshaped how an entire generation of later painters understood color and perception, freeing them from the long-standing obligation to reproduce every visible contour. [■] The movement's influence spread rapidly well beyond France within just a few decades. However, some critics still argue that this narrow focus on momentary sensation left Impressionism unable to convey the deeper structure and enduring meaning that more deliberate, carefully planned compositions achieve.

questions.context:
A suspension bridge is a structure whose roadway hangs from steel cables draped between two tall towers. This design allows it to span far greater distances than a bridge supported by piers alone. The main cables carry the weight of the deck, transferring the load along their curved length toward the towers and the massive anchorages at each end.

These anchorages are the key to the whole system. Each main cable pulls inward with enormous tension, and only a heavy anchor block buried in rock or concrete can resist being dragged toward the center of the span. From the main cables hang slender vertical wires, called suspenders, that grip the deck at regular intervals and hold it level against the constant pull of gravity.

Wind poses the subtlest danger to such slender spans. A steady gust can set a lightweight deck oscillating, and if the rhythm matches the bridge's natural sway, the motion grows until the structure tears itself apart. Modern engineers therefore shape the deck like an aircraft wing and add internal trusses, letting air slip past smoothly so that the completed span holds steady in strong wind.

questions.context:
A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without itself being consumed or permanently altered by it. Many industrially valuable reactions proceed far too slowly at ordinary room temperatures to be commercially useful. Raising the temperature can indeed accelerate them, but heating enormous volumes of raw material demands costly energy and can destroy delicate products, leaving manufacturers facing an expensive and wasteful dilemma.

Catalysts resolve this fundamental problem by offering the reacting molecules an easier, lower-energy chemical pathway. A catalyst binds the reactants to its surface, weakening their internal bonds so that they combine using far less energy than the direct route would require. Because the catalyst itself emerges unchanged, a small quantity can process an enormous stream of material, driving the reaction rapidly at gentle, economical temperatures.

These advantages have made catalysts central to modern chemistry, from refining crude fuels to manufacturing agricultural fertilizer. Yet real catalysts gradually lose their power as unwanted impurities coat their active surfaces, a process known as poisoning. Chemists are now designing more resilient materials that resist such contamination, and this steady progress promises even more efficient and sustainable industrial reactions.

options.D: Why fertilizer production demands enormous energy input

questions.context:
A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without itself being consumed or permanently altered by it. Many industrially valuable reactions proceed far too slowly at ordinary room temperatures to be commercially useful. Raising the temperature can indeed accelerate them, but heating enormous volumes of raw material demands costly energy and can destroy delicate products, leaving manufacturers facing an expensive and wasteful dilemma.

Catalysts resolve this fundamental problem by offering the reacting molecules an easier, lower-energy chemical pathway. A catalyst binds the reactants to its surface, weakening their internal bonds so that they combine using far less energy than the direct route would require. Because the catalyst itself emerges unchanged, a small quantity can process an enormous stream of material, driving the reaction rapidly at gentle, economical temperatures.

These advantages have made catalysts central to modern chemistry, from refining crude fuels to manufacturing agricultural fertilizer. Yet real catalysts gradually lose their power as unwanted impurities coat their active surfaces, a process known as poisoning. Chemists are now designing more resilient materials that resist such contamination, and this steady progress promises even more efficient and sustainable industrial reactions.
questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, what causes a catalyst to lose its effectiveness?

options.A: Rising temperatures that slowly melt its surface
options.B: Repeated washing that dissolves the active metal
options.C: Long storage that dries out the material
options.D: Impurities that gradually coat its active surface

questions.subtopic: how sleep consolidates memories in the brain
questions.context: Sleep does a lot more than just rest your body. One of its most important jobs is helping the brain lock in what you learned during the day. Neuroscientists call this process memory consolidation. When you first learn something, that memory is fragile and stored in a region called the hippocampus, which acts like a temporary holding area. During sleep, the brain replays and gradually transfers those memories to the neocortex, where they become more stable and long-lasting. Here's a study that shows this nicely. Researchers taught two groups of people a list of word pairs. One group then slept for a few hours, while the other stayed awake. When they were all tested later, the group that slept remembered far more pairs. And it wasn't just about being tired. Brain recordings showed that during deep sleep, the same neural patterns that fired while learning the words fired again, as if the brain were rehearsing them. Now, think about what this means for how you study. Pulling an all-nighter before an exam might let you cram more facts in, but without sleep, your brain never gets the chance to file those facts away properly. This is why spacing out your studying across several nights works so much better than one long session. The sleeping brain isn't idle at all. It's quietly sorting, strengthening, and organizing everything you took in. So the next time you feel guilty about going to bed instead of studying more, remember that sleep itself is part of the learning.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How sleep helps the brain strengthen and store new memories
options.B: Why students feel tired after staying awake all night
options.C: How the hippocampus controls physical rest and recovery
options.D: The best techniques for memorizing long lists of words

questions.subtopic: how dual-process theory explains fast and slow thinking
questions.context: When you make a decision, it can feel like a single act of thinking. But cognitive psychologists argue that two very different systems are actually at work. This is the core of dual-process theory. The first, often called System 1, is fast, automatic, and effortless. It's what lets you recognize a friend's face instantly or answer two plus two without pausing. The second, System 2, is slow, deliberate, and demanding. It's the system you use to solve a tricky math problem or plan a route through an unfamiliar city. Here's a famous puzzle that shows the difference. A bat and a ball cost one dollar and ten cents together. The bat costs one dollar more than the ball. How much is the ball? Most people blurt out ten cents. That's System 1 jumping in with an answer that feels right. But if you slow down and check, the ball actually costs five cents. System 2 has to override the quick guess. Now, why does this matter? System 1 is not a flaw. It handles the vast majority of daily decisions efficiently, and we couldn't function without it. The problem comes when we rely on it in situations that really call for careful reasoning. Recognizing when to shift gears, to deliberately slow down and engage System 2, is a skill that good decision-makers develop. Next time, we'll look at how these two systems interact when people face financial choices, and why even experts fall back on fast thinking under pressure.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why simple arithmetic problems confuse most adults
options.B: A theory that two mental systems drive our thinking
options.C: How to train the brain to solve puzzles more quickly
options.D: The history of research into human decision-making errors

questions.subtopic: why a spinning object stays balanced through angular momentum
questions.context: Try to balance a bicycle that isn't moving, and it topples right over. But once the wheels are spinning, the same bicycle stays upright with almost no effort. The physics behind this is angular momentum. Any spinning object possesses angular momentum, a quantity that depends on how fast it spins, how much mass it has, and how that mass is distributed around the axis. And here's the crucial property: angular momentum resists change. A spinning object tends to keep its axis pointed in the same direction unless an outside force pushes on it. We call this gyroscopic stability. Consider a spinning top. When it spins quickly, it stands upright, seemingly defying gravity. Gravity is certainly pulling on it, but instead of simply falling, the top's axis slowly traces a circle, a motion called precession. The faster it spins, the more stubbornly it holds its orientation. The same principle keeps a moving bicycle wheel resisting a tip to either side. This idea has enormous practical value. Ship stabilizers use heavy spinning wheels to keep vessels from rolling in rough seas. Spacecraft carry devices called reaction wheels that spin up or slow down to rotate the craft precisely, without burning any fuel. Even a thrown football is deliberately given a spin so it holds a stable nose-forward path through the air. So the wobble-free ride you get on a fast bicycle isn't about balance in the ordinary sense. It's angular momentum quietly doing the work, resisting any change to the spinning wheel's orientation.

options.A: Why bicycles are easier to ride than to build
options.B: How gravity causes spinning objects to fall over
options.C: How angular momentum keeps spinning objects stable
options.D: The different ways ships are protected from rough seas

questions.subtopic: how ancient amphitheaters were designed for natural acoustics
questions.context: Stand at the center of an ancient Greek theater, speak in a normal voice, and someone in the very top row, dozens of meters away, can hear you clearly. No microphones, no speakers. How did builders two thousand years ago achieve this? The answer lies in acoustic design, and it was remarkably sophisticated. Take the theater at Epidaurus in Greece, built in the fourth century BCE. It seats roughly fourteen thousand people, and its acoustics are still astonishing today. For a long time, scholars assumed the steep, semicircular slope was the whole secret. The rising rows of seats do help, since sound carries better when it travels up an open incline without obstacles. But researchers discovered something more clever. The limestone seats themselves act as an acoustic filter. The rows form a corrugated surface that reflects sound, and this surface naturally dampens low-frequency background noise, like the murmur of the crowd or wind, while it lets the higher frequencies of a human voice pass through and reach the back. In effect, the seating filters out the very sounds that would drown out a speaker. What's striking is that the Greeks almost certainly discovered this by trial and error, not by understanding sound waves the way we do now. They refined the shape over generations until it simply worked. Modern acousticians, studying these ruins with sensitive instruments, are still learning from them. It's a reminder that careful observation and patient adjustment can produce solutions that rival, and sometimes surpass, our theoretical designs.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why ancient Greek theaters were built to seat large crowds
options.B: How modern instruments measure sound in old buildings
options.C: The history of the theater at Epidaurus and its builders
options.D: How ancient theaters were designed to carry sound naturally
